pattern: Because the count of magical numbers up to any candidate value grows monotonically with that value, and inclusion-exclusion gives that count directly from division and the least common multiple, binary searching on the candidate value pins down the exact nth one.
hints: For any candidate value x, the count of magical numbers at most x is how many multiples of a it has, plus how many multiples of b, minus how many multiples of their least common multiple (to avoid double-counting numbers divisible by both).
That count only ever grows as the candidate value grows, so binary search for the smallest candidate whose count reaches n.
The candidate value itself can be found exactly with ordinary integer arithmetic; only the very final answer needs to be reduced modulo one billion and seven.

edge_inputs.description: a large n with small divisors
both divisors at the maximum bound
one divisor is a multiple of the other, so their least common multiple equals the larger one
the divisors share a large common factor
the smallest possible n with the smallest possible divisors

stress.description: random small-to-moderate n with random divisor pairs
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    a = rng.randint(2, 40000)
    b = rng.randint(2, 40000)
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 1000000000)))
    return [n, a, b]

stress.description: random n reaching close to the maximum constraint
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    a = rng.randint(2, 40000)
    b = rng.randint(2, 40000)
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 1000000000)))
    return [n, a, b]
